questions.q: Identify the observation that first led Andres Freund to discover the xz backdoor.
options: An antivirus scanner flagged the liblzma library as malicious
His SSH logins were taking about half a second longer than normal (roughly 600ms instead of 100ms)
A colleague reported that the xz utility was producing corrupted compressed files
Automated tests in a Linux distribution failed during a routine release
questions.explanation: Freund, running database performance tests, noticed that logging in over SSH was costing about half a second more than it should — roughly 600ms versus a usual 100ms. No scanner caught it; the discovery came from one curious engineer wondering why a routine login suddenly burned extra processor time. That tiny performance cost is the only reason the attack was caught before wide release.

questions.q: State what kind of software xz is and why poisoning it was so dangerous.
options: It is a web server, so poisoning it would expose every website's traffic
It is an encryption library, so poisoning it would break all secure connections
It is a small file-compression utility woven so deeply into Linux that, through a chain of dependencies, it reaches the SSH login server
It is an operating-system kernel, so poisoning it would crash machines on boot
questions.explanation: xz is a tiny, unglamorous compression tool (squeezing data smaller), but it is everywhere in Linux and — through a chain of dependencies — connects to the SSH login server that guards remote access to most internet servers. Its danger came from its position of trust, not from being exciting software. It is not an encryption library or a kernel; that confusion misses why a 'dull little compression tool' mattered so much.

questions.q: Outline how the attacker, using the name 'Jia Tan', gained the authority to ship official xz releases.
options: They stole the maintainer's credentials in a phishing attack and took over the account
They spent years making small helpful contributions to earn trust, while sock-puppet accounts pressured the lone maintainer to hand over control
They bought the project from the original maintainer for a large sum of money
They exploited a vulnerability in the project's version-control system to grant themselves access
questions.explanation: The attack began with a relationship, not code. From 2021, Jia Tan made modest, reliable contributions over months and years, while fake personas (sock puppets) on the mailing list complained the lone maintainer was too slow and pressured him to add co-maintainers. Worn down, Lasse Collin gradually handed over real authority. This was social engineering of a community, not a stolen password.

questions.q: Explain why the malicious code was hidden in the build process and disguised as test data rather than placed in the source code.
options: Build scripts run faster than source code, so the backdoor would activate sooner
Test data is automatically encrypted, which hid the payload from antivirus tools
The attacker knew reviewers read source code but rarely scrutinise build machinery and almost never inspect binary test files, so the poison assembled only in the shipped release
Source files are too small to contain a backdoor, so the payload had to live in larger test files
questions.explanation: The malice was hidden in the build process and disguised as innocent-looking corrupted 'test data' precisely because reviewers read source code but rarely scrutinise the machinery that compiles it, and almost never inspect binary test files. A hidden script extracted the real payload at build time and wove it into the final library, so the public source looked completely clean. The hiding place was chosen to defeat human review, not for speed or encryption.

questions.q: Describe what the finished backdoor did once it was active inside the SSH login process.
options: It silently copied every password typed at login and sent them to a remote server
It watched for a specific cryptographic key only the attacker held and, when seen, let them run commands with full control while behaving normally for everyone else
It disabled the SSH server entirely so that no one could log in to affected machines
It mined cryptocurrency in the background, which explained the extra half-second of processing
questions.explanation: The backdoor lay in wait inside SSH and watched for a secret signal — a specific cryptographic key only the attacker possessed. To everyone else the system behaved completely normally, but the keyholder could run their own commands, bypassing all the usual checks, with full control — a skeleton key to a huge share of the internet's servers. It did not steal passwords or disable SSH; it granted targeted, hidden access.

questions.q: Why is the xz incident described as the cleanest example of a software supply-chain attack?
options: Because the attacker broke into systems from the outside by guessing administrator passwords
Because the attacker poisoned an upstream dependency that victims would willingly invite in as part of a routine update, making the software they depend on the attack surface
Because the attack only affected the single machine on which the malicious code was written
Because it spread automatically between machines like a self-replicating worm
questions.explanation: A supply-chain attack does not break down your door; it poisons something upstream that you willingly invite inside, with your blessing, as part of a routine update. Your software is a vast tree of other people's code, and every link in that tree is a way in — so the software you depend on is itself an attack surface. This is the opposite of an attacker forcing their way in from outside, which is what the tempting 'guessing passwords' option describes.

questions.q: Distinguish between how open source helped the attacker and how it ultimately defeated them.
options: Open source helped because the code was secret, and defeated the attacker because it was later made public
Open source relies on the goodwill and labour of volunteers who can be pressured and exhausted, yet its openness — many eyes able to read the code — is how the attack was eventually caught
Open source helped because there was no maintainer at all, and defeated the attacker because a company audited the code
Open source had no role either way; the attack and its discovery were both purely technical
questions.explanation: The same model cuts both ways. Open source rests on the labour of volunteers who can be befriended, pressured, and exhausted — which the attacker exploited socially — but its openness means anyone can read the code, and that is exactly how the malice was finally caught in the open. The attacker defeated the human community, not the technology. Open-source code is public, not secret, so the option claiming it was 'secret' misstates the model.

questions.q: Explain what this case study reveals about when in the software development life cycle security must be maintained.
options: Security is a phase completed at the start of a project and then ticked off
Security only matters during testing, just before a release ships
Security must live across the entire life cycle, including the long, quiet maintenance years, because that is where a patient adversary chooses to strike
Security is only a concern for brand-new projects that have not yet built a user base
questions.explanation: The xz backdoor was not a flaw in how the code was originally written — it was malice injected during maintenance, into a mature, trusted project, through the slow erosion of who controlled it. Security is not a phase you finish at the start and tick off; it has to live across the whole development life cycle, especially the boring, established stage where a patient adversary expects no one to be watching. Treating security as a start-of-project task is exactly the mindset this attack relied on.

questions.q: A development team imports a popular open-source library that one volunteer has maintained for years, and a new contributor recently gained the power to publish its releases. Recommend the most appropriate control to guard against an xz-style attack.
options: Run an antivirus scan over the library's published source code before each update
Verify that the shipped binary is reproducible from the reviewed source and review who has authority to publish releases, not just the source code itself
Switch to a closed-source paid library, since commercial software cannot be backdoored
Only update the library once per year to reduce how often new code enters the project
questions.explanation: The xz backdoor lived in the build process, so reviewing only the source — or scanning it with antivirus — would have missed it entirely. The defences this attack was designed to slip past are build integrity, reproducible builds (checking the shipped binary genuinely matches the reviewed source), and scrutiny of who holds release authority. Closed source is not immune and updating less often does not address a poisoned release, so those options give false confidence.

questions.q: Identify the official catalogue identifier and severity rating assigned to the xz backdoor.
options: CVE-2024-3094, rated as severe as the scale allows (CVSS 10.0)
CVE-2021-44228, rated medium severity
CVE-2024-1086, rated low severity
CVE-2023-3094, rated high but not maximum severity
questions.explanation: After Andres Freund's disclosure on 29 March 2024, the vulnerability was catalogued as CVE-2024-3094 and rated as severe as the scale allows — a CVSS score of 10.0. The maximum rating reflects how close it came: poisoned versions had already reached the testing branches of major Linux distributions, weeks away from rolling out to everyone. The other identifiers belong to different, unrelated vulnerabilities.
